title: Operation Ann

jokes.text: Josef Stalin Dies? That's right, Mom. And you replaced him. Not in terms of genocide. Just in terms of, you know, being on Earth.
jokes.setup: Reading newspaper headline from mother's birth date
jokes.punchline: Casual clarification that she replaced Stalin only by existing, not by committing genocide
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered with Leslie's characteristic earnest obliviousness. The casual genocide qualifier is brilliantly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my God, look at this headline from the day I was born. 'Leslie Knope Replaces Stalin as Most Evil Person in History.' Well, technically I replaced him just by being born that day. I didn't actually commit genocide or anything.

jokes.text: Dear Congress. It's Leslie again.
jokes.setup: Leslie suggesting Galentine's Day should be a national holiday
jokes.punchline: Implied she's already written to Congress multiple times about various topics
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie characterization - her civic obsessiveness in four words. Maximum character integration with incredible economy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Galentine's Day should be a national holiday. I've already written to Congress about it. Multiple times. Along with my other suggestions.

jokes.text: I have several men in rotation. One's waiting for me out in the car. Don't worry. I rolled down the window.
jokes.setup: Donna talking about her romantic life
jokes.punchline: Treating her date like a dog left in a car
jokes.explanation: Classic Donna - confident and slightly dehumanizing treatment of men, delivered with perfect casual authority.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I treat my dates like a dog left in a car. I crack the window, tell them I'll be right back, and then I go to brunch with my girlfriends for four hours.

jokes.text: Well, last week, he was supposed to buy gas, but instead, he bought novelty cookie cutters. Now, everything we eat is shaped like a dinosaur. He's amazing.
jokes.setup: Asked how married life with Andy is going
jokes.punchline: Describing objectively terrible decision-making as 'amazing'
jokes.explanation: Perfect April - deadpan delivery of Andy's absurdity with genuine affection underneath the sarcasm.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: It's amazing. He describes objectively terrible decision-making as 'amazing.' Like, he'll say, 'I just spent our entire savings on a synthesizer I don't know how to play,' and I'm like, 'That's amazing, Andy.'

jokes.text: It's really hard to say 'congrats' without sounding sarcastic.
jokes.setup: Ann trying to congratulate her friends on their relationships while being single
jokes.punchline: Meta-observation about the difficulty of sounding genuine
jokes.explanation: Relatable awkwardness of single person at couple events. Good observational humor with mild cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You guys are so lucky to have each other. I mean that. I'm not just saying that because I'm single and bitter.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Although, full transparency, I am single and bitter.

jokes.text: Seriously, I really hope you guys are all very happy. Damn it.
jokes.setup: Ann trying again to sound genuine
jokes.punchline: Immediately failing again and cursing her inability
jokes.explanation: Effective callback that escalates the awkwardness. Simple but well-executed character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I'm really glad we're friends. I mean that sincerely.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Ugh, why do I always sound like I'm reading from a hostage video?

jokes.text: Oh, Ann. You beautiful spinster. I will find you love.
jokes.setup: Leslie feeling sorry for single Ann
jokes.punchline: Oxymoronic 'beautiful spinster' and mission statement
jokes.explanation: Leslie's characteristic mix of affection and mild insult, with her meddling nature on full display.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ann, you're a beautiful spinster. And I'm going to make it my personal mission statement to find you a man.

jokes.text: Great attitude, Ron. Sorry. I was talking to these ribs.
jokes.setup: Leslie complimenting Ron's attitude
jokes.punchline: Ron clarifies he was talking to his food, not participating
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron moment - his disengagement and love of meat in one efficient exchange. Great misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I really admire your positive attitude about this meal.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I wasn't talking to you. I was having a conversation with my food.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, I thought you were just being nice.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I was being nice. To the food. You're not invited to that conversation.

jokes.text: Ann's not totally hideous. Why does she need our help?
jokes.setup: Leslie asking everyone to find dates for Ann
jokes.punchline: April's backhanded assessment of Ann's attractiveness
jokes.explanation: Classic April - the faint praise insult delivered with complete sincerity. Pure character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Ann is a beautiful, talented, powerful musk ox.

jokes.text: Just tell us the damn word. Effervescent.
jokes.setup: Leslie giving increasingly elaborate descriptions of Ann's ideal man
jokes.punchline: April cutting to the chase, then Leslie using pretentious word
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between April's impatience and Leslie's overwrought vocabulary. 'Effervescent' is perfectly Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ann's ideal man would be someone who's well-read, culturally aware, someone who appreciates the finer things in life—

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: A guy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: —someone with gravitas, panache, a certain je ne sais quoi—

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: A guy.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: —someone with the strength of Hercules, the wisdom of Socrates, the charm of Cary Grant—

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: A guy.

jokes.text: Quick question about Ann. Does anyone know if she has any Indian in her? No one respond. No one say anything. Why? I'm just curious if Ann has a little Indian in her. Silence. I don't think she does. Would she like some?
jokes.setup: Tom asking seemingly innocent question about Ann's heritage
jokes.punchline: Sexual innuendo becoming increasingly obvious despite Leslie's attempts to stop it
jokes.explanation: Classic Tom inappropriate humor. Gets laughs but relies on juvenile sexual innuendo. Authentic to character but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: So Ann, where are you from originally?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Mansfield, Ohio.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh nice. I bet you had some great experiences growing up there. Did you ever go deep into the woods?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom...

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What? I'm just asking if she explored the local area. I hear the terrain gets pretty thick and hard to penetrate.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, we should probably—

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: And I imagine a girl like Ann really knew how to handle a big, long... hiking trail.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Tom, stop it right now.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, she probably got pretty sweaty working those trails, maybe needed a good stiff drink afterwards—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We're done here.

jokes.text: Yachter Otter? Yep. Two months ago, I have a dream about a playboy otter lost at sea, and you make him real? I love it.
jokes.setup: Ben receiving Valentine's gift
jokes.punchline: Leslie created a toy based on Ben's specific bizarre dream
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd concept with perfect Leslie thoughtfulness. 'Playboy otter lost at sea' is genuinely creative and weird.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I made you something special for Valentine's Day.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, what is this?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's an action figure based on your dream where you're a merman who works at the Parks Department.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: How did you know about that?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You talk in your sleep. Also, I made him anatomically accurate.

jokes.text: Oh. A bomb? It is a cryptex, like in that movie The Da Vinci Code, which was the first movie that you and I ever watched on Starz HD.
jokes.setup: Ben seeing cryptex gift
jokes.punchline: Leslie's hyper-specific romantic memory about cable movie watching
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's attention to romantic minutiae. The Starz HD detail is nicely specific.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: What is this?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's a cryptex. You have to solve the puzzle to open it. Inside is a note about the time we watched that cable movie at 2 AM when we couldn't sleep, and you fell asleep during the opening credits, so I just watched you sleep for forty-five minutes instead of the actual movie.

jokes.text: Millicent Gergich has literally torn my heart from my body, and replaced it with a thick slab of sadness.
jokes.setup: Andy asking if Chris is thinking about his ex
jokes.punchline: Chris's overwrought metaphorical description of heartbreak
jokes.explanation: Perfect Chris - his earnest, overwrought way of describing emotions. 'Thick slab of sadness' is memorably absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Are you thinking about your ex?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: My heart is a vast, barren wasteland. A once-fertile valley now ravaged by drought and pestilence. The crops have withered, the livestock have fled, and all that remains is the echo of what once was—a hollow wind whistling through the ruins of my soul.

jokes.text: Tom, this is a publicly funded couples dance. I don't think it's appropriate for people to be 'getting wet with sound.'
jokes.setup: Tom suggesting DJ Bluntz for the dance
jokes.punchline: Chris's earnest concern about inappropriate DJ slogan for government event
jokes.explanation: Good contrast between Tom's party mentality and Chris's government propriety. The quote marks around the slogan work well.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: We should get DJ Bluntz to play the dance.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: DJ Bluntz? Isn't his slogan 'Get Bluntz-ed'?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's his thing.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I don't think that's appropriate for a government-sponsored event.

jokes.text: Have you tried... That's a four-letter word. Add an 'S'?
jokes.setup: Ben struggling with five-letter cryptex code
jokes.punchline: Andy suggesting inappropriate four-letter word, then helpfully suggesting adding S
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy - inappropriate suggestion followed by hilariously literal solution. In character but relies on implied profanity.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I can't figure out this five-letter code. I've tried everything.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh! Try D-U-M-B-S!

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That spells 'dumbs.' That's not a word.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Well, what if you add an 'S' to the end?

jokes.text: Did you try... Yes. Why is that everyone's first suggestion?
jokes.setup: Andy about to suggest the same inappropriate word
jokes.punchline: Ben's exasperation that everyone suggests the obvious dirty word
jokes.explanation: Effective callback to establish pattern, but still relies on implied profanity for humor.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What if we call it the Pawnee Fucking... I mean, the Pawnee F-word...

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Yes, Andy. Everyone immediately thinks of that word. That's why we can't use it.

jokes.text: Jerry, well done. I put an ad on Craigslist. 'Man seeking man for a night of casual fun.' Enrico, he responded right away.
jokes.setup: Jerry proudly announcing he found a date for Ann
jokes.punchline: Revealing he accidentally posted in men-seeking-men section
jokes.explanation: Pure Jerry incompetence - well-intentioned but completely wrong execution. The pride in his voice makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I found a date for Ann! I posted on a dating website.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Turns out I accidentally posted in the men-seeking-men section.

jokes.text: You hired a male escort. A what? Please get your gigolo out of here.
jokes.setup: Leslie realizing Jerry's mistake
jokes.punchline: Jerry's innocent confusion about what he's done
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Jerry's incompetence. His genuine confusion sells the character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Jerry, what did you do?

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What? What did I do?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You filed the permit under the wrong department code.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I did? I thought I... I was just trying to help.

jokes.text: April hates Valentine's Day. And brunch and outside and smiling. She's weird.
jokes.setup: Ben apologizing for ruining their Valentine's Day
jokes.punchline: Andy listing progressively more basic things April hates
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation from specific (Valentine's Day) to absurdly basic (smiling). Andy's loving delivery makes it endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I'm so sorry I ruined Valentine's Day.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's okay, man. April hates Valentine's Day anyway.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: She also hates: fancy restaurants, flowers, the color red, heart-shaped anything, romance, emotions, and people.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Basically, she hates Valentine's Day.

jokes.text: Are you kidding me? No. You brought Orin? Ann is not some weird morose mummy. Offense intended, Orin.
jokes.setup: April bringing her weird friend as Ann's date
jokes.punchline: Leslie's direct insult with polite clarification
jokes.explanation: Great subversion of 'no offense' politeness. Leslie being directly mean is unusual and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, your friend is a little weird.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I mean that as a compliment.

jokes.text: What about a wedding where you used to go out with the groom and you're the only one there without a date, so the bride makes you dance to Single Ladies by yourself?
jokes.setup: Ann saying she can't imagine anywhere more depressing than couples dance while single
jokes.punchline: Hyper-specific, humiliating wedding scenario that clearly happened to her
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly specific cringe scenario that escalates from bad to mortifying. The detail suggests it's autobiographical.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I can't imagine anywhere more depressing than a couples dance while single.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Well, except maybe a wedding where the bride specifically requests that all single guests sit together at a table in the corner, and then during the father-daughter dance, the DJ announces 'Let's see which single ladies want to join them!' and literally shines a spotlight on your table.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: And then your ex shows up with his new girlfriend, and she's wearing the same dress you were going to wear.

jokes.text: My dentist is 80, and he's gay and he's taken.
jokes.setup: Listing potential dates for Ann
jokes.punchline: Each detail makes the option progressively more impossible
jokes.explanation: Good escalating list where each detail eliminates the possibility. Economical and effective.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Okay, so we got Tuesday at 7, but he's a little weird about dairy.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Wednesday at 8, but he only speaks Klingon.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Thursday at 9, but he's currently incarcerated.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: And Friday at 10, but he's dead.

jokes.text: What's this? 'P Hut.' Pizza Hut. Hold on. Hi. How cute are you?
jokes.setup: Leslie desperately seeking dates for Ann
jokes.punchline: Calling Pizza Hut to ask about employee attractiveness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie desperation move. Her directness about asking strangers about their attractiveness is beautifully in character.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Hi, yes, I'm calling Pizza Hut. Do you have any cute delivery drivers? No? What about in the kitchen? Anyone with a strong jawline? Okay, well, can you just describe your employees to me? I'm trying to set my best friend up on a date.

jokes.text: But something wicked with a book is my ex-wife from the library. Which means I'm the one that succumbs.
jokes.setup: Ron solving Leslie's riddle
jokes.punchline: Casually referring to his ex-wife as 'something wicked'
jokes.explanation: Classic Ron - matter-of-fact disdain for his ex-wife delivered without emotion. Good character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Something wicked this way comes.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's Tammy Two.

jokes.text: 'Follow me to the sheltered snow. Only 22 clues left to go.' 22? Well, this is the woman I've chosen to love.
jokes.setup: Ben finding another clue in the scavenger hunt
jokes.punchline: His resigned acceptance of Leslie's elaborate romantic gesture
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment showing Ben's acceptance of Leslie's intensity. The resignation mixed with love works well.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Of course there's another clue. Of course there is.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I'm going to be doing this until midnight, aren't I?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: You know what? It's fine. This is fine. I love her. I can do this.

jokes.text: Nobody here compares to Millicent. Except maybe Jerry. Technically, they share 50% of the same DNA.
jokes.setup: Tom suggesting Chris rebound with someone at the dance
jokes.punchline: Chris considering Jerry as romantically viable due to genetic similarity to his daughter
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully disturbing logic that's both scientifically accurate and completely inappropriate. Dark humor that pushes boundaries.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: You should rebound with someone at the dance.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I don't know, Tom. I'm not really in the headspace for that.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Come on, man. You need to get back out there.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Well, maybe... I mean, Jerry does share similar genetic material with my daughter.

jokes.text: You really suck at this. Okay. Why don't you just tell me where it is, and I can get out of here? That would take all the fun out of it.
jokes.setup: Ben struggling to find Leslie's clue
jokes.punchline: Museum employee enjoying Ben's suffering more than helping
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment with sadistic museum employee. Minor character gets a good comedic beat.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Excuse me, do you work here? I'm looking for something that was left for me.

dialogue.speaker: Museum Employee
dialogue.line: I might be able to help you. What are you looking for?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: It's a clue. My girlfriend left it somewhere in the museum.

dialogue.speaker: Museum Employee
dialogue.line: Well, that's interesting. I'm going to need more information than that.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Okay, well, it would be in an envelope with my name on it.

dialogue.speaker: Museum Employee
dialogue.line: I'm going to enjoy watching you search for this.

jokes.text: Not as beautiful as my sister, but you know the law.
jokes.setup: Jeff complimenting Ann's beauty
jokes.punchline: Implying there's a law preventing him from dating his more beautiful sister
jokes.explanation: Bizarre logic that's funny in its absurdity. The casual reference to 'the law' about incest is darkly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Ann, you're so beautiful. It's actually illegal for me to date you.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: What? That doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Jeff
dialogue.line: Yeah, there's a law. No man is allowed to date a woman more beautiful than his sister. It's to protect guys like me.

jokes.text: I'm Harris. I heard you were desperate for a man piece. We in business?
jokes.setup: Tom introducing Ann to a potential date
jokes.punchline: Harris's crudely direct proposition
jokes.explanation: Crude humor that gets laughs through shock value rather than cleverness. Serviceable but not sophisticated.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ann, I'd like you to meet Harris. Harris, this is Ann.

dialogue.speaker: Harris
dialogue.line: Hey Ann. You single?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Um, yes, actually—

dialogue.speaker: Harris
dialogue.line: You wanna get out of here and go back to my place?

jokes.text: Try 308. I'm going to have to pass right now. Your mistake, mama.
jokes.setup: Tom mentioning Harris has been to 200 Phish concerts
jokes.punchline: Harris correcting to higher number, then being smugly confident after rejection
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Harris as even worse than initially presented. His confidence after rejection is perfectly obnoxious.

dialogue.speaker: Harris
dialogue.line: It's actually 247.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: That's not a flex.

dialogue.speaker: Harris
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: Where to begin? I'm an amateur juggler. Nope, you shouldn't have begun there. Get out. No one's trying to get with jugglers.
jokes.setup: Tom asking what Bill brings to the table
jokes.punchline: Tom immediately shutting down juggling as completely undateable
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and immediate destruction. The universal truth about jugglers being undateable is hilariously delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: What do you bring to the table?

dialogue.speaker: Bill
dialogue.line: I can juggle.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Well, that's completely undateable.

jokes.text: Well, they always say break glass in case of an emergency.
jokes.setup: Ron retrieving Leslie's clue from emergency axe case
jokes.punchline: Ron treating scavenger hunt as legitimate emergency
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron deadpan delivery. His literal interpretation of emergency protocol for romantic purposes is beautifully in character.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: This is an emergency. I need this axe.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, no, that's my clue!

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: A scavenger hunt clue is the definition of an emergency.

jokes.text: What if she was my personal best? No. Your best is still ahead of you. I am 44 years old. You don't look a day over 30. Most people say 25. Who says that? A lot of people.
jokes.setup: Chris worrying about Millicent being his peak
jokes.punchline: Chris's increasingly specific age compliments that he clearly solicits
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Chris's vanity and need for validation. The escalating age estimates reveal his fishing for compliments.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: You know, I'm worried that Millicent was my peak. Like, what if I never accomplish anything as great as being a father to her?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Chris, that's ridiculous. You're going to do amazing things.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: You really think so?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know so. You're an incredible person.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: But like, specifically though, how incredible? Like on a scale of...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're the most incredible 42-year-old man I've ever met.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: 42 and three months.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Right, 42 and three months.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: And what about compared to other men my exact height and build?

jokes.text: And you tossed her out like day-old chowder.
jokes.setup: Leslie pointing out Chris dated perfect Ann but let her go
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific and unappetizing metaphor for breakup
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully specific and unappetizing metaphor that's unexpectedly funny. 'Day-old chowder' is memorably gross.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You dated the perfect woman and you let her go. That's like... that's like ordering the best pizza in the world and then just leaving it on the roof.

jokes.text: You're going to throw them on the roof again so I can't leave. You know me too well.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking for Ann's car keys for suspicious reason
jokes.punchline: Ann immediately knowing Leslie's history of this exact behavior
jokes.explanation: Great callback that reveals Leslie's past desperate friend behavior. Ann's immediate recognition shows their relationship history.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ann, can I borrow your car keys?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: What did you do?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Nothing! Why does something have to be wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Because every time you ask to borrow my car, you've either gotten it towed, or driven it into a lake, or used it to transport something illegal.

jokes.text: Ann, you tricky bastard.
jokes.setup: Leslie realizing Ann is getting ready for a secret date
jokes.punchline: Leslie's affectionate but accusatory response to being deceived
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Leslie's mix of admiration and betrayal. The affectionate insult works well.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ann! Ann, what is happening right now? You're wearing makeup. You never wear makeup. And you're wearing the good bra. I can tell because you're standing differently. Where are you going?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I'm just going out.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You're going on a date! Oh my God, you're going on a date and you didn't tell me! We tell each other everything. I'm hurt. I'm actually hurt. This is a betrayal of the highest order.

jokes.text: Weird stick. Might be a clue? No.
jokes.setup: Andy helping Ben find clues
jokes.punchline: Andy's completely wrong interpretation of random object
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy - finding meaning where none exists. Simple but effective character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay Ben, I found a clue! This bottle cap is definitely a tiny frisbee for a very small dog.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Andy, that's a bottle cap.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Exactly! A bottle cap frisbee!

jokes.text: Ann would never do anything to piss you off, Leslie. You guys are such close friends. It's lame.
jokes.setup: April defending Ann to Leslie
jokes.punchline: April calling their close friendship 'lame' while defending it
jokes.explanation: Perfect April - showing she cares while maintaining her disdainful persona. The 'lame' qualifier is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Look, Ann is my best friend. I know she's lame, but she's MY lame.

jokes.text: Ron, you're a genius. Li'l Sebastian. Yep. At first, you did not understand what made this tiny horse so special. And now, you love him more than I do.
jokes.setup: Ben having breakthrough about cryptex code
jokes.punchline: Ben's conversion to Li'l Sebastian appreciation as the key to his relationship with Leslie
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback to major character development. Ben's journey from Li'l Sebastian skeptic to believer as romantic breakthrough is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait... Li'l Sebastian. That's it. That's the answer to everything.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: What are you talking about?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie loves Li'l Sebastian more than anything. If I want to understand her, I need to understand why she cares so much about a tiny horse.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's the dumbest thing I've ever heard.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: No, Ron. It's actually genius. I'm going to become a Li'l Sebastian enthusiast.

jokes.text: I really tried to make that hard. Yeah. I'm very impressed with you.
jokes.setup: Ben solving Leslie's elaborate puzzle
jokes.punchline: Leslie admitting she intentionally made it difficult, then praising Ben for succeeding
jokes.explanation: Good relationship moment showing Leslie's competitive nature even in romance. Her pride in his success is sweet.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, I have to admit something. I intentionally made that puzzle really difficult.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Wait, what? Why would you do that?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Because I wanted to see if you were smart enough to solve it. And you did! You're so smart, Ben. I'm impressed.

jokes.text: Screw romantic dinners. Let's go rub it in their face. God, I love you so much.
jokes.setup: Leslie wanting to confront Chris and Ann
jokes.punchline: Ben enthusiastically choosing confrontation over romance, Leslie's joy at his support
jokes.explanation: Great relationship moment showing Ben understanding Leslie's priorities. His immediate enthusiasm for petty confrontation is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Should I confront Chris and Ann about what they're doing?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Yes! Absolutely, confront them. I'm with you 100%. Let's do this right now.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Really? You're not worried about the drama?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Drama? I don't care about drama. I care about justice. And you. Mostly you being right about things.

jokes.text: April. Hey. April. Tom and Ann are on a date. I know. What do you mean you know? How do you know? Because I set them up.
jokes.setup: Leslie discovering unexpected couple
jokes.punchline: April casually revealing she orchestrated it
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that reveals April's hidden matchmaking. Her casual delivery of the twist works well.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, what? How did you two even meet?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I set it up. You were spending too much time at work, so I may have mentioned to them that you were single and sad.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April! You can't just... I mean, that's... did you really do that?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Yeah. Also, I told them you volunteer on weekends and own seventeen cats. You're welcome.

jokes.text: This is a small loser town with loser people, and Tom's, like, at least semi-cool.
jokes.setup: April explaining her matchmaking logic
jokes.punchline: Backhanded compliment that insults everyone while barely praising Tom
jokes.explanation: Perfect April - insulting everyone while making practical point. 'Semi-cool' as high praise from her is brilliantly in character.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm trying to set Tom up with someone equally mediocre. You know, like pairing a mid-tier wine with a mid-tier cheese. They're both disappointing on their own, but together they might distract each other from how forgettable they are.

jokes.text: You're a very nice person. No. Yes, you are. A very nice person. Bye.
jokes.setup: Leslie recognizing April's kindness
jokes.punchline: April's complete rejection of being called nice
jokes.explanation: Classic April dynamic - her horror at being called nice despite clearly caring. Well-established character pattern.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, you're actually really nice.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa. Let's pump the brakes on that. I'm not nice. I'm mean. I say mean things. That's my thing.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: But you do nice things.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I do nice things, but I'm not nice. There's a difference. I'm like a serial killer, except instead of murdering people, I help them organize their closets.

jokes.text: Yeah, but I love passionate speakers and Italian men. Doesn't mean I love Mussolini.
jokes.setup: Leslie confused about loving Tom and Ann separately vs together
jokes.punchline: Comparing relationship logic to fascist dictator
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd leap from dating logic to historical fascism. The specificity and wrongness of the analogy is perfectly Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I love Tom and I love Ann, but I don't love Tom and Ann together. It's like... it's like how I love every country, but if they all tried to live in the same house, it would be a nightmare. It's like fascism.

jokes.text: Scottish? White. Whatever you are.
jokes.setup: Ben asking about Leslie's attraction to different ethnicities
jokes.punchline: Leslie's increasingly broad and eventually just racial categories
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Leslie's romantic enthusiasm getting less specific. The final 'whatever you are' is endearing.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: So what's your type? Like, what ethnicity are you usually attracted to?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, well, I like guys who are smart and funny and caring...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: So I guess my type is... European?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Actually, no. Caucasian.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, I mean... human.

jokes.text: Then, we go back to my place and snuggle up like little bunnies. Yeah, this was a mistake.
jokes.setup: Tom suggesting alternate date plan
jokes.punchline: Tom's weird baby-talk seduction immediately killing the mood
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom move - ruining romantic moment with inappropriate over-sharing. Ann's immediate retreat is the perfect response.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Hey Ann, what if we skip dinner and I just talk to you in baby voice all night?

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: I absolutely do not want to solve a series of riddles and clues, each more intricate than the last. You understand what I'm saying? Yeah. I got it, Ron. Good. I do want that. Please do that for me.
jokes.setup: Ron emphatically stating he doesn't want elaborate birthday celebration
jokes.punchline: Complete reversal revealing he actually does want exactly that
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection showing Ron's secret romantic side. The elaborate setup makes the reversal more satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't want some big, elaborate birthday celebration. No fuss, no gifts, no hoopla. Just leave me alone.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ron, we already planned the whole thing.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Cancel it.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Too late. Everyone's already here with decorations and a mariachi band.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: A mariachi band? How many musicians?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Seven.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Well... that's a good number. And the gifts — are they wrapped nicely?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Beautifully.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: What did you get me?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A woodworking set and a case of scotch.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: This might be the best day of my life.

tmdb_overview: Leslie works to find Ann a Valentine's date. Ron helps Ben with a scavenger hunt, and Chris becomes depressed over his love life.
insight: Operation Ann escalates character chaos into 49 jokes—Ann becomes collateral damage in a perfectly dense ensemble outbreak.
